text.title: claude-best-practices Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for claude-best-practices. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use Haiku for formatting, routing, classification, and simple extraction tasks. Use Sonnet for generation, code, and analysis. Use Opus for complex reasoning, architecture, and strategy tasks.
rule.rationale: Using Opus for a simple routing decision wastes ~10x the budget. Using Haiku for complex reasoning produces lower-quality output that may require re-work.

rule.statement: Pin exact model IDs in production configs (e.g., "claude-sonnet-4-20250514") rather than aliases ("claude-sonnet-4").
rule.rationale: Aliases can update to a newer model version without notice. A silent model upgrade can change output format or behavior, breaking downstream parsing.

rule.statement: Log the model actually used in every response, not just the model requested. Log this when implementing fallback logic.
rule.rationale: Fallback to a cheaper model is invisible to the orchestrator unless explicitly logged. Downstream agents may produce worse outputs without knowing the model was downgraded.

rule.statement: Always set max_tokens explicitly on every Messages API call.
rule.rationale: The default max_tokens is low and silently truncates structured output mid-JSON, producing invalid payloads that are hard to debug because the API returns HTTP 200.

rule.statement: When stop_reason == "max_tokens" on a structured output call, the JSON is truncated and invalid. Retry with higher max_tokens; do not parse the response.
rule.rationale: A truncated JSON response from a structured output call will raise a parse error downstream. The correct action is to retry with more headroom, not to handle the parse error.

rule.statement: Place the most stable content at the top of the system prompt with cache_control: ephemeral. Put dynamic instructions last.
rule.rationale: Cache uses prefix matching. Dynamic content at the top busts the cache on every call. Stable content at the top maximizes the proportion of input tokens that are cached.

rule.statement: Never inject timestamps, request IDs, or user-specific values before the cached block.
rule.rationale: Even a single character difference in the prefix causes a cache miss. You pay the write cost (25% above regular) with no read benefit.

rule.statement: Measure cache_read_input_tokens / input_tokens ratio per pipeline run. Target above 0.70.
rule.rationale: Below 70% cache hit rate, write overhead starts eroding the 90% read savings. Low hit rate indicates the cached prefix is being modified per call.

rule.statement: Use Batch API for workloads that tolerate up to 24-hour latency to receive 50% cost reduction.
rule.rationale: Batch API cuts cost in half with no other code changes. The only requirement is that the workload is not real-time: offline enrichment, nightly analysis, evaluation suites.

rule.statement: Use client.messages.count_tokens() for pre-flight checks before paying for large-input calls.
rule.rationale: Token counting is cheap (no generation cost) and prevents paying full generation cost for inputs that exceed the context limit, which fail with an API error.

rule.statement: Parse the retry-after header from RateLimitError instead of guessing the backoff interval.
rule.rationale: The Anthropic API includes a retry-after header with the exact wait time. Guessing with exponential backoff may wait too long or too short, degrading throughput unnecessarily.

rule.statement: Implement exponential backoff with jitter for 5xx server errors; retry up to 3 times before raising.
rule.rationale: 5xx errors are transient. A single retry captures most transient failures. More than 3 retries risks cascading delays in multi-step pipelines.

rule.statement: In multi-worker orchestrators, use a shared rate-limit token bucket rather than per-process backoff.
rule.rationale: Per-process backoff does not account for other workers consuming quota simultaneously. A shared bucket prevents all workers from hammering the API at the same time after a backoff period.

rule.statement: When falling back from Sonnet to Haiku on rate limiting, log the actual model used in the response.
rule.rationale: The orchestrator needs to know which model produced a given output. Silent fallback produces unexpected quality variations that are impossible to audit without this log.

rule.statement: Use AsyncAnthropic with asyncio.gather for concurrent batch calls. Do not spawn threads; the SDK is async-native.
rule.rationale: The Anthropic Python SDK is built on httpx with native async support. Using threads adds overhead and can introduce race conditions. asyncio.gather is the correct concurrency primitive.
